title: A mixed finite element formulation of triphasic mechano-electrochemical theory for charged, hydrated biological soft tissues
abstract:
INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL FOR NUMERICAL METHODS IN ENGINEERING
Int. J. Numer. Meth. Engng. 45, 1375}1402 (1999)
A MIXED FINITE ELEMENT FORMULATION OF
TRIPHASIC MECHANO-ELECTROCHEMICAL THEORY
FOR CHARGED, HYDRATED BIOLOGICAL SOFT TISSUES
D. N. SUN1, W. Y. GU2, X. E. GUO1, W. M. LAI1 AND V. C. MOW1,*
1Orthopaedic Research ¸aboratory, Department of Mechanical Engineering and Orthopaedic Surgery,
Columbia ;niversity, New >ork, N> 10032, ;.S.A.
2Department of Biomedical Engineering, ;niversity of Miami, ;.S.A.
SUMMARY
An equivalent new expression of the triphasic mechano-electrochemical theory [9] is presented and a mixed
"nite element formulation is developed using the standard Galerkin weighted residual method. Solid
displacement us, modi,ed electrochemical/chemical potentials e8, e`and e~ (with dimensions of concentra-
tion) for water, cation and anion are chosen as the four primary degrees of freedom (DOFs) and are
independently interpolated. The modi"ed Newton}Raphson iterative procedure is employed to handle the
non-linear terms. The resulting "rst-order Ordinary Di!erential Equations (ODEs) with respect to time are
solved using the implicit Euler backward scheme which is unconditionally stable. One-dimensional (1-D)
linear isoparametric element is developed. The "nal algebraic equations form a non-symmetric but sparse
matrix system. With the current choice of primary DOFs, the formulation has the advantage of small
amount of storage, and the jump conditions between elements and across the interface boundary are
satis"ed automatically. The "nite element formulation has been used to investigate a 1-D triphasic
stress relaxation problem in the con"ned compression con"guration and a 1-D triphasic free swelling
problem. The formulation accuracy and convergence for 1-D cases are examined with independent
"nite di!erence methods. The FEM results are in excellent agreement with those obtained from the other
methods. Copyright ( 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
KEY WORDS:
soft tissue; triphasic mechano-electrochemical theory; "nite element method
INTRODUCTION
Soft connective tissues are important constituents of the human body and their functions play
critical roles in various normal and pathological conditions [1]. Although the morphology and
the location of hydrated soft tissues such as articular cartilage, intervertebral disk, growth plate,
vascular vessel, and skin are diverse, they share similarities in compositions. All such biological

 Figure 1. A schematic diagram of the structure of the charged hydrated articular cartilage
materials are multiphasic. In general, they are basically formed from a "brous network of
structure proteins (such as collagen and/or elastin with varying amounts of proteoglycons (PG),
"bronectin and other glycoproteins), abundant interstitial water, soluble electrolytes such as
NaCl and CaCl2, etc., and cells residing in the interstitial space [2, 3]. The extracellular matrix
(ECM) of connective tissues is porous and permeable [4]. Often, there are negatively or positively
charged groups on the ECM, or the solid &skeleton' of the soft tissues. These charges on the solid
ECM are called "xed charges because their mobility is much less than that of freely mobile ions
dissolved in the interstitial water. Articular cartilage, for example, is such a hydrated, charged soft
tissue. It consists mostly of dense network of type II collagen "bers and PG aggrecans; this
"ber-gel forms the porous-permeable matrix. Within the interstitial #uid, at physiological pH and
ionic strength, PG aggrecans, with abundant sulphate groups on their glycosaminoglycan (GAG)
chains, become negatively charged and attract mobile cation ions (e.g. Na`) in the interstitial
#uid to maintain the electroneutrality of the tissue as a whole (Figure 1). The mechano-
electrochemical properties of such tissues depend on the relative amount of the tissue compo-
nents, ultrastructure organization of the solid matrix and the mechano-electrical interaction of
the components within the extracellular matrix. Many mechano-electrochemical coupling phe-
nomena such as ion-induced swelling, streaming potential and current, anomalous osmosis and
electro-osmosis observed in such charged biological tissues are attributed to the electrostatic and
frictional interactions between the "xed charges and mobile ions.
A number of theoretical models have been developed to investigate the #ow-dependent
viscoelastic behavior (e.g. [4}7]) and mechano-electrochemical behavior (e.g. [8}11]) of charged
hydrated soft tissues, using (1) the continuum mixture theory approach ([12, 13, 59], also see
recent review paper by de Boer [14]); (2) the consolidation theory approach [15, 16]; or (3) the
combination of biphasic mixture theory [4] or the consolidation theory [15, 16] with a phenom-
enological transport theory [17]. Due to the complexity of the above theories for the multiphasic
materials, it is impossible to obtain an analytical solution for most of the practical boundary-
value problems associated with speci"c experiments. Thus, the "nite element method, because of

TRIPHASIC MECHANO-ELECTROCHEMICAL THEORY
FOR CHARGED, HYDRATED BIOLOGICAL SOFT TISSUES
D. N. SUN1, W. Y. GU2, X. E. GUO1, W. M. LAI1 AND V. C. MOW1,*

"nite element formulation is developed using the standard Galerkin weighted residual method. Solid
displacement us, modi,ed electrochemical/chemical potentials e8, e`and e~ (with dimensions of concentra-
tion) for water, cation and anion are chosen as the four primary degrees of freedom (DOFs) and are
independently interpolated. The modi"ed Newton}Raphson iterative procedure is employed to handle the
non-linear terms. The resulting "rst-order Ordinary Di!erential Equations (ODEs) with respect to time are
solved using the implicit Euler backward scheme which is unconditionally stable. One-dimensional (1-D)
linear isoparametric element is developed. The "nal algebraic equations form a non-symmetric but sparse
matrix system. With the current choice of primary DOFs, the formulation has the advantage of small
amount of storage, and the jump conditions between elements and across the interface boundary are
satis"ed automatically. The "nite element formulation has been used to investigate a 1-D triphasic
stress relaxation problem in the con"ned compression con"guration and a 1-D triphasic free swelling
problem. The formulation accuracy and convergence for 1-D cases are examined with independent
"nite di!erence methods. The FEM results are in excellent agreement with those obtained from the other
methods. Copyright ( 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
soft tissue; triphasic mechano-electrochemical theory; "nite element method
Soft connective tissues are important constituents of the human body and their functions play
critical roles in various normal and pathological conditions [1]. Although the morphology and
the location of hydrated soft tissues such as articular cartilage, intervertebral disk, growth plate,
vascular vessel, and skin are diverse, they share similarities in compositions. All such biological

BB1412, New York, NY 10032, U.S.A. E-mail:vcm1@columbia.edu

Figure 1. A schematic diagram of the structure of the charged hydrated articular cartilage
materials are multiphasic. In general, they are basically formed from a "brous network of
structure proteins (such as collagen and/or elastin with varying amounts of proteoglycons (PG),
"bronectin and other glycoproteins), abundant interstitial water, soluble electrolytes such as
NaCl and CaCl2, etc., and cells residing in the interstitial space [2, 3]. The extracellular matrix
(ECM) of connective tissues is porous and permeable [4]. Often, there are negatively or positively
charged groups on the ECM, or the solid &skeleton' of the soft tissues. These charges on the solid
ECM are called "xed charges because their mobility is much less than that of freely mobile ions
dissolved in the interstitial water. Articular cartilage, for example, is such a hydrated, charged soft
tissue. It consists mostly of dense network of type II collagen "bers and PG aggrecans; this
"ber-gel forms the porous-permeable matrix. Within the interstitial #uid, at physiological pH and
ionic strength, PG aggrecans, with abundant sulphate groups on their glycosaminoglycan (GAG)
chains, become negatively charged and attract mobile cation ions (e.g. Na`) in the interstitial
#uid to maintain the electroneutrality of the tissue as a whole (Figure 1). The mechano-
electrochemical properties of such tissues depend on the relative amount of the tissue compo-
nents, ultrastructure organization of the solid matrix and the mechano-electrical interaction of
the components within the extracellular matrix. Many mechano-electrochemical coupling phe-
nomena such as ion-induced swelling, streaming potential and current, anomalous osmosis and
electro-osmosis observed in such charged biological tissues are attributed to the electrostatic and
frictional interactions between the "xed charges and mobile ions.
A number of theoretical models have been developed to investigate the #ow-dependent
viscoelastic behavior (e.g. [4}7]) and mechano-electrochemical behavior (e.g. [8}11]) of charged
hydrated soft tissues, using (1) the continuum mixture theory approach ([12, 13, 59], also see
recent review paper by de Boer [14]); (2) the consolidation theory approach [15, 16]; or (3) the
combination of biphasic mixture theory [4] or the consolidation theory [15, 16] with a phenom-
enological transport theory [17]. Due to the complexity of the above theories for the multiphasic
materials, it is impossible to obtain an analytical solution for most of the practical boundary-
value problems associated with speci"c experiments. Thus, the "nite element method, because of
Copyright ( 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
its ability to handle complicated geometry and boundary conditions, becomes the best choice of
The "nite element models for the biphasic theory [4, 18}20] were developed by Spilker and
co-workers. The penalty formulation [21, 22], mixed penalty formulation [23], hybrid formula-
tion [24] and mixed u}p formulation [25, 26] were proposed. With the progress of biphasic "nite
element method, a variety of problems such as friction e!ect on the uncon"ned compression [27],
indentation analysis under "nite deformation [28] and non-isotropic analysis [29] have been
solved. Some challenging topics such as automated-mesh-adaptivity [30] and joint contact
problems [31, 32] are being addressed. Recently, some investigators began to describe the
contributions of ions using "nite element method [11, 33}35].
It should be pointed out here that, according to the interface jump conditions [36], the #uid
velocity can be discontinuous across the interface between two biphasic layers with di!erent
porosities or between a biphasic layer and a single phase layer. In other words, with di!erent
porosities on both sides, the #uid velocity has no unique value right at the interface. Thus, if the
#uid velocity is chosen as one of the primary degrees of freedom, the "nite element formulation
will be limited only to the problems in which the intrinsic porosity of the material is continuous.
A triphasic mechano-electrochemical theory has been developed by Lai et al. [9], which
encompasses both the biphasic view and physico-chemical view of the soft tissues. It is also
consistent with and subsumes many previously derived mixture and phenomenological transport
theories pertaining to speci"c aspects of the charged-hydrated tissues (e.g. [37}43]). It has been
used to describe mechano-electrochemical behavior of the soft tissues such as cartilage and
intervertebral disk [9, 44}48]. The objectives of this paper are: (1) to derive a concise form of the
triphasic mechano-electrochemical theory [9, 48] suitable for "nite element formulation; (2) to
develop a "nite element formulation for the triphasic theory; (3) to test the accuracy and the
convergence of the formulation by comparison of "nite element solutions with independent "nite
di!erence solutions. As a "rst attempt to develop the "nite element formulation for the triphasic
theory and without losing generality, only the monovalent (1:1) electrolyte and in"nitesimal
deformation are considered here.
THE TRIPHASIC MECHANO-ELECTROCHEMICAL THEORY
The triphasic mechano-electrochemical theory [9] considers a charged hydrated soft tissue as
a mixture consisting of: (1) an intrinsically incompressible, porous-permeable-charged solid phase
(superscript s); (2) an intrinsically incompressible interstitial #uid phase (superscript w); and (3)
ion phase with two monovalent ion species, i.e., anion (!) and cation (#). Recently, this theory
has been extended to include multiple species of ions [48]. We will only consider 1:1 electrolyte
case here while the method can be extended to include the multiple ion cases without di$culty.
This theory is strictly based on the laws of continuum mechanics and the second law of

In the triphasic theory, by neglecting gravitational and magnetic e!ects, the gradient of the

forces between one phase and another. Thus, the momentum equations are

AN EQUIVALENT FORMULATION FOR THE TRIPHASIC THEORY
In the following, an equivalent formulation for the triphasic theory will be derived. This new
formulation permits easy implementation for "nite element realization and has the advantage
that the jump conditions between elements and across the interface boundary are satis"ed
automatically even for inhomogeneous tissues with discontinuous material properties.

THE FINITE ELEMENT FORMULATION

It should be noted that the current formulation is similar to the mixed u}p "nite element
formulation for the saturated porous media (for biphasic mixture) in terms of treatment of the
pressure term [55, 56, 25, 26]. The main di!erence is that this paper deals with a new triphasic
mechano-electrochemical theory in which the ion concentrations and electrical portential are
considered as well as the solid deformation and #uid pressure. If the ion concentration and
Copyright ( 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
electrical potential e!ects are neglected, the triphasic theory reduces to the biphasic theory [9],
the water chemical potential essentially reduces to #uid pressure and the current triphasic "nite
element formulation reduces to the biphasic u}p formulation. In addition, our formulation
involves the "rst-order derivatives of the chemical potential for water e8, electrochemical
potentials for cation e` and anion e~ as well as the solid displacement us. The same highest-order
derivatives of all primary DOFs lead to the same continuity requirement on them; therefore, we
do not require di!erent order shape functions for the quantities us, e8, e` and e~.
Standard assembly operations can be performed to sum up the element contributions, which
produces a "rst-order ordinary di!erential equation with respect to time:
The "rst-order di!erential equations can be solved using the "nite di!erence method. The implicit
Euler backward scheme is used from the consideration of its unconditional stability,
where i is the index of iteration within each time step. Then the "nal algebraic equation system is
Within each time step, non-linear iterations are repeated until the incremental values *d(i)
small enough (less than the tolerance), i.e. the convergence criteria is
The "nite element formulation is implemented for one dimensional (1-D) cases using 2-node
linear isoparametric element for solid displacement "eld and modi"ed electrochemical/chemical
potentials. A 1-D free swelling problem (i.e. no applied tractions) and triphasic stress relaxation
(i.e. displacement-controlled) problem were investigated using this FEM formulation. The FEM
results were checked and compared with those obtained by the "nite di!erence method (FDM).
To investigate the mechano-electrochemical coupling phenomena of charged, hydrated soft
tissues, a transient free swelling problem was studied using our FEM (Figure 2(a)). The shape
functions N and H used in this one-dimensional problem are isoparametric linear functions as
shown in Figure 2(a). Assuming a frictionless lateral boundary, only axial motion is possible and
the problem is reduced to a 1-D problem. Initially, a tissue sample was at equilibrium with the

Figure 3 is a schematic diagram of the con"ned compression stress relaxation experiment.
A soft tissue with a thickness h is placed in a con"ning chamber (Figure 3(a)). The shape functions
N and H used in this one-dimensional problem are similar to the ones used above. The boundary
conditions at bottom are the same as those in the free swelling problem mentioned above. In this
problem, however, at the top surface (x"h) a ramp displacement (Figure 3(b)) is applied through
a rigid porous-permeable (i.e. free draining) loading platen. The electrochemical/chemical poten-
tials are continuous across this boundary. The concentration c*0 of the external solution is kept
constant during the test (i.e. neglecting the e!ect of ion transfer). The solid displacement, ion
concentrations, #uid and ion #uxes within the tissue, and the resultant stress are calculated using
our FEM. Note that the initial equilibrium free swelling state is used as the reference con"gura-
tion for both free swelling and stress relaxation problems.

A total of 50 uniform elements were used for this problem. The material parameters used
for FEM calculation were h"0)5 mm, j4#2k4"0)5 MPa, B8"0, D`"0)5]10~9 m2/s,

basic scales for non-dimensionalization were taken as c0"0)1M and D0"D`. Initially, the
external solution concentration was 0)15 M. It decreased linearly to 0)125 M within 10 s and then
remained constant (Figure 2(b)). The time step was 1 s. Immediately after dilution of the external
solution, the tissue begins to swell. Figure 4 shows the history of the solid displacement at the
surface. This swelling result is consistent with our experimental observation and those of other
investigations [57]. To check the accuracy of our FEM calculation, the result was compared with
that obtained using FDM with 101 grid points. As shown in Figure 4, the agreement between two
calculations is excellent ([max (DuFDM!uFEMD/uFDM)](0)1per cent).
Figure 5 shows the cation concentration distributions within the tissues during the free swelling
process. Again, the FEM results are in excellent agreement with those calculated by FDM. To
investigate the e!ects of the FCD and ion di!usivities on the free swelling behavior of charged
hydrated soft tissues, we calculated the solid displacement at surface as a function of FCD
(Figure 6) as well as a function of the ratio of ion di!usivities (Figure 7). As shown in Figure 6, the
equilibrium swelling strain increases as the FCD increases. At early time an interesting anomal-
ous displacement in the tissue swelling is noted as the FCD is decreased. That is, for certain values
of FCD (e.g. FCD"0)05 mEq/ml), the tissue shrinks initially ((210 s). Figure 7 shows the
surface displacement history as a function of D`/D~ (where D~"0)8]10~9 m2/s is "xed). As
shown in Figure 7, the increase of D`/D~ facilitates tissue swelling. Interestingly, when
D`/D~'1, instead of swelling monotonically, there is an &overshoot' in the tissue swelling, but
"nally the tissue reaches the same equilibrium state as for the case where D`/D~)1. These
anomalous phenomena have underlying physical mechanisms and are believed to be related to
the negative osmosis e!ects [47]. The detailed analysis will be presented in a separate paper.

SUMMARY AND CONCLUSION REMARKS
In this study we developed a new equivalent expression of the triphasic mechano-electrochemical
theory [9]. For this equivalent form, we derived a mixed "nite element using the standard
Galerkin weighted residual method. In this FEM formulation, solid displacement us, modi"ed
electrochemical/chemical potentials e8, e` and e~ for water, cation and anion are the four
primary DOFs and are independently interpolated. The modi"ed Newton}Raphson iterative
procedure was employed to handle the nonlinear terms. The resulting "rst order ODE with
respect to time was solved using the implicit Euler backward scheme that is unconditionally
stable. Since the system is not self-adjoint, the "nal algebraic equation system is not symmetric
but it is still sparse. A 1-D linear isoparametric element was developed and the formulation
accuracy and convergence have been tested with two illustrative examples.
MIXED FINITE ELEMENT FORMULATION
Copyright ( 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
With the current choice of primary DOFs, the formulation has the advantage of small amount
of storage (only one vector and three scalar variables). At each node there are only four unknowns
in 1-D cases and there are only six unknowns for 3-D cases. Also, the jump conditions between
elements and across the interface boundary are automatically satis"ed. Moreover, the current
formulation does not require special treatments (e.g. the penalty method) for this intrinsically
The 1-D free swelling and stress relaxation problems were studied. The solid displacement, ion
concentrations, #uid and ion velocities were calculated using our FEM and compared with those
using FDM. The results show that there is an excellent agreement between two methods. The
numerical results are also consistent with many of the experimental observations on charged
hydrated soft tissues reported in the literature [4, 19, 57]. No locking and spurious modes such as
checkerboard phenomenen are found in the calculation. Through these sample cases, it is shown
that our FEM formulation is capable of solving the complex triphasic problems of charged
hydrated soft tissues under di!erent types of mechanical, electrical and physicochemical loading
conditions. With the success of 1-D cases, the multi-dimensional problems will be investigated in
